title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardians of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Kaitiakitanga i te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will investigate the environmental and cultural significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a vital waterway flowing through our rohe that holds deep significance for Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that traditionally grew along its banks, and it has been both a source of traditional kai (eels, kōura) and a site for mahinga kai practices for generations.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three ways human activity has impacted Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, using evidence from field observations.
Compare and contrast Western environmental impact assessment methods with the Ngāti Kahungunu concept of kaitiakitanga through a structured Venn diagram.
Collaboratively develop a kaitiakitanga action plan proposing one specific measure to enhance the mauri (life force) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku.
Present findings and proposed actions to an audience using appropriate te reo Māori terminology related to the awa (stream) and kaitiakitanga.

activities.title: Whakatau me te Whakawhanaungatanga: Welcoming and Connecting to the Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge the mauri of the awa. Students engage in a 'see, think, wonder' observation exercise at a designated safe point along Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, sketching the environment and noting initial thoughts on its health and usage.

activities.title: He Kohikohinga Kōrero: Gathering Stories of the Awa
activities.description: In small groups, students analyze historical photos of the awa from the local marae archive and listen to a pre-recorded short kōrero (talk) from a local kaumātua about the traditional significance and historical health of the awa. They create a timeline of key changes.

activities.title: Ngā Tūraru me ngā Pānga: Identifying Pressures and Impacts
activities.description: Using a simplified field guide, students conduct a visual assessment of the stream bank (e.g., evidence of erosion, litter, plant life). They then map observed human activities (e.g., nearby land use, tracks) and hypothesize their potential impacts on the water quality and ecosystem.

activities.title: Te Rerekētanga o ngā Ariā: Contrasting Lenses (Kaitiakitanga vs. EIA)
activities.description: Using a card-sorting activity, students work in pairs to categorize statements and concepts under the headings of 'Kaitiakitanga' (e.g., intergenerational responsibility, mauri) and 'Western EIA' (e.g., mitigation, measurement). The class then collaborates to complete a large Venn diagram showing overlaps and differences.

activities.title: Te Mahere Mahi Kaitiaki: Developing a Kaitiakitanga Action Plan
activities.description: Based on their investigations, groups brainstorm and design a specific, actionable kaitiakitanga project for the awa (e.g., organising a community planting day for native riparian plants, designing educational signage, writing a letter to the council). They prepare a brief presentation outlining their plan.

resources: Kaumātua from local Ngāti Kahungunu marae (e.g., Ruahapia Marae) for guidance and kōrero
Historical photographs and maps of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku from the MTG Hawke's Bay Tai Ahuriri or local marae archives
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' (SHMAK) simplified field guides for students
Te Ara - The Encyclopedia of New Zealand online entry 'Te Awa: Māori and Rivers'

tasks: Group presentation of the 'Kaitiakitanga Action Plan' for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Individual written reflection connecting their proposed action to the principles of kaitiakitanga and their initial field observations

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies
Social Studies L4: Understand how people’s management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Vision: Confident, connected, actively involved, lifelong learners
Principles: Cultural diversity, Community engagement, Future focus, Inclusion
Values: Innovation, inquiry, and curiosity; Ecological sustainability; Community and participation
